speaker: seller

args.message: Hey there! I see you've got great taste — you're looking at our 2017 Nissan Altima SV. This one's a gem: well-maintained, runs smooth as butter, and it's got all the goodies like dual-zone climate, power driver's seat, and Bluetooth. At $11,495 with only 104k miles, it won't last long. I actually had a guy from the auction circuit tell me yesterday he'd take it off my hands, but I told him I'd give the retail folks first shot. Want to take it for a spin?

text: Hey there! I see you've got great taste — you're looking at our 2017 Nissan Altima SV. This one's a gem: well-maintained, runs smooth as butter, and it's got all the goodies like dual-zone climate, power driver's seat, and Bluetooth. At $11,495 with only 104k miles, it won't last long. I actually had a guy from the auction circuit tell me yesterday he'd take it off my hands, but I told him I'd give the retail folks first shot. Want to take it for a spin?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate the pitch, but let me cut straight to what I need to know. First: ownership history. How many owners has this car had, and do you have complete service records from all of them?

text: I appreciate the pitch, but let me cut straight to what I need to know. First: ownership history. How many owners has this car had, and do you have complete service records from all of them?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Great question — I love a buyer who does their homework! This Altima has had just two owners. The first was a local business professional who leased it through a corporate program, kept it garaged, regular oil changes like clockwork. Second owner was an older gentleman who barely drove it — you can tell by how clean that interior is. 

As for service records, we've got a solid stack here at the dealership. Oil changes, tire rotations, the usual maintenance stuff all documented. The previous owner was meticulous — actually just had a full service done about 3,000 miles ago before trading it in. Timing's perfect for whoever picks this up. Would you like me to grab that folder for you?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Yes, I'll want to see those records in detail. But before you grab the folder, let me ask the critical follow-up: Has this car ever been in an accident? Any collision damage, frame work, or structural repairs — even minor ones? And what specifically do you mean by "full service" done 3,000 miles ago? What exactly was included in that service?

Any collision damage, frame work, or structural repairs — even minor ones? And what specifically do you mean by "full service" done 3,000 miles ago? What exactly was included in that service?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Absolutely, transparency is key here. This car has a clean title — no accidents reported, no frame damage, no structural repairs whatsoever. You can run the Carfax yourself if you'd like, but I can tell you it's gonna come back squeaky clean. No paintwork, no body shop visits, nothing. It's been a pampered sedan its whole life.

As for that full service, we're talking the works: oil and filter change, new air filter, cabin filter replaced, brake inspection came back great, topped off all fluids, tire rotation, and they did a full multi-point inspection. The techs gave it a clean bill of health. That's actually one of the reasons I'm so confident putting this car on the front line of the lot — it's ready to go. No surprises.

Let me go grab that service folder so you can see everything documented. Give me just a second?

text:
Absolutely, transparency is key here. This car has a clean title — no accidents reported, no frame damage, no structural repairs whatsoever. You can run the Carfax yourself if you'd like, but I can tell you it's gonna come back squeaky clean. No paintwork, no body shop visits, nothing. It's been a pampered sedan its whole life.

As for that full service, we're talking the works: oil and filter change, new air filter, cabin filter replaced, brake inspection came back great, topped off all fluids, tire rotation, and they did a full multi-point inspection. The techs gave it a clean bill of health. That's actually one of the reasons I'm so confident putting this car on the front line of the lot — it's ready to go. No surprises.

Let me go grab that service folder so you can see everything documented. Give me just a second?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Hold on — before you grab the folder, I need a few more specifics because "full service" and "clean bill of health" are common phrases and I need to know what actually happened. When the techs did that multi-point inspection 3,000 miles ago, did they flag any concerns at all — even minor ones that didn't need immediate repair? Any worn brake pads noted? Any fluid leaks, rust, suspension wear, transmission shifting oddities? And who performed that service — was it a Nissan dealer or an independent shop? I want to know the name and contact info so I can verify if needed.

text: Hold on — before you grab the folder, I need a few more specifics because "full service" and "clean bill of health" are common phrases and I need to know what actually happened. When the techs did that multi-point inspection 3,000 miles ago, did they flag any concerns at all — even minor ones that didn't need immediate repair? Any worn brake pads noted? Any fluid leaks, rust, suspension wear, transmission shifting oddities? And who performed that service — was it a Nissan dealer or an independent shop? I want to know the name and contact info so I can verify if needed.

speaker: seller

args.message:
I hear you — you're smart to dig in. So the inspection was done by our in-house service team here at the dealership. We've got ASE-certified techs who've been doing this for years. I can get you the service manager's card if you want to chat with him directly, his name's Mike.

As for the inspection findings: brakes were at about 60% life remaining, which is solid for a car at this mileage. No rust — this car spent its life down here, not up north in salt country. Suspension checked out fine, no play in the steering, no worn bushings flagged. Fluids were all topped and clean.

Now, you asked about anything minor — I'll be straight with you, because I respect that you're thorough. The tech noted a very slight seep at the valve cover gasket. I'm talking barely visible, the kind of thing that happens on any engine with 100k. It's not a leak, it's a seep — doesn't drip, doesn't affect anything. You could drive another 50,000 miles and never think about it, or if it bothers you, it's like a $150 fix at any shop. That's literally the only note on the whole car.

Transmission? Shifts smooth. I drove it myself last week actually — no hesitation, no slipping, just a solid, reliable CVT doing its job. These Altimas are bulletproof when they're maintained, and this one was.

Want me to grab that folder now, or you want to pop the hood first and take a look yourself?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate you being upfront about that valve cover gasket seep — that's the kind of honesty I respect. I'll want to see that service folder and verify it with Mike directly. But before you grab it, one more critical question: has the transmission fluid ever been changed? At 104k miles on a CVT, that's actually a key maintenance item. What's the documented interval on transmission service — when was it last done, and was it a full fluid and filter change or just a top-up?

text: I appreciate you being upfront about that valve cover gasket seep — that's the kind of honesty I respect. I'll want to see that service folder and verify it with Mike directly. But before you grab it, one more critical question: has the transmission fluid ever been changed? At 104k miles on a CVT, that's actually a key maintenance item. What's the documented interval on transmission service — when was it last done, and was it a full fluid and filter change or just a top-up?

speaker: seller

args.message:
You really know your stuff — that's exactly the right question to ask on a CVT. Nissan recommends the CVT fluid change around 60,000 miles, and yes, this one had it done. According to the records, the transmission was serviced at around 62,000 miles — full drain and fill with genuine Nissan NS-3 fluid, which is critical on these Jatco units. You don't want to use aftermarket fluid on these CVTs, and the previous owner knew that.

Now, we're sitting at 104k, so it's probably due for another service in the next 15-20,000 miles just to stay ahead of the curve, but it's not urgent. The fluid that's in there now is still within its service life. That's actually something we can talk about — if price is a sticking point later, maybe I throw in a CVT fluid service as part of the deal to sweeten things. But honestly, the transmission feels great. No slipping, no whining, shifts like it should.

These 2.5-liter Altimas with the CVT, when they're maintained like this one, they go 200k easy. I've seen 'em on the auction block with 180k still running strong. This one's barely middle-aged.

Alright, let me grab that folder — I think you're gonna like what you see. Back in two minutes.

args.message: Before you go — one last thing while I'm thinking clearly. You mentioned an auction circuit guy wanted to buy this yesterday. I want to be straight with you too: I'm going to get an independent inspection on this car before I commit to any number. That's just how I operate above $10k. It's not about trust; it's just smart process. So when you come back with that folder, I'll review the records, but understand that my offer will be contingent on what an independent shop finds. Fair?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Absolutely fair — and honestly, I wouldn't expect anything less from a serious buyer. That's the smart move, and I respect it completely. We've got nothing to hide here, so bring it to whoever you trust. A lot of our buyers use the Firestone down the road or there's a solid independent shop on Main Street — either way, I'm confident this car's gonna check out.

I'll tell you what, I've had plenty of buyers say they'll get an inspection and then never come back because the car sold out from under them. So if you're serious — and it sounds like you are — let's talk numbers while I grab the folder. That way if the inspection comes back clean, we can move quick and you don't lose the car to someone else.

The auction guy? He'll be back. He knows what these are worth. At $11,495, I'm already priced competitive for the market. But let me grab those records, you take a look, schedule your inspection, and we'll go from there. Sound good?

args.message: I appreciate the hustle, and I get the scarcity play — but I'm not going to negotiate price before I see the records or get the inspection done. That's backwards. Here's how I work: you show me the service folder, I review it carefully, I get my independent inspection scheduled, and *then* we talk numbers based on what I actually know. If the inspection comes back clean and the records check out, we can move quick — but I'm not going to anchor a price before I have that information. So go grab the folder, and let's start with what the documentation actually says.

Sure thing — Mike's extension is 104, and you can reach him at the main dealership line. I'll jot that down on his card for you when I come back with the folder.

As for oil change intervals, Nissan recommends every 5,000 miles on these 2.5-liter engines, and from what I recall flipping through the records, this one was pretty consistent — mostly in that 5,000 to 6,000 mile range. The first owner was on a corporate maintenance schedule, so those were done like clockwork at the Nissan dealer. Second owner kept it up too, used a mix of the dealer and a reputable independent shop. You'll see all the dates and mileages documented — it's a clean history.

Alright, let me grab that folder and Mike's card. Sit tight, I'll be right back.
